expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of ionic radius trends across the periodic table, exploring how atomic size changes when atoms gain or lose electrons to form ions, and how these changes affect chemical bonding, crystal structures, and biological function.

core_concept.principle: Cations are smaller than their parent atoms (electron loss reduces electron-electron repulsion), while anions are larger than their parent atoms (electron gain increases electron-electron repulsion). Ionic radius increases down groups and varies across periods based on charge and electron configuration.
core_concept.explanation: When atoms form ions, their size changes dramatically due to changes in electron configuration and electron-electron repulsion. Cations form when atoms lose electrons, reducing the electron cloud and allowing remaining electrons to be pulled closer to the nucleus. Anions form when atoms gain electrons, increasing electron-electron repulsion and expanding the electron cloud. These size changes affect lattice energies in ionic compounds, solubility, biological ion transport, and material properties. Understanding ionic radius trends enables prediction of compound stability, crystal structures, and chemical reactivity patterns.

image.alt: Diagram comparing atomic and ionic radii for metals and nonmetals
image.caption: Cations are smaller than parent atoms; anions are larger than parent atoms. The change reflects electron loss or gain and resulting changes in electron-electron repulsion.

sections.introduction: Ionic radius is the measure of an ion's size in a crystal lattice, representing the effective distance from the nucleus to the outer boundary of the electron cloud in an ionic compound. Unlike atomic radius, ionic radius depends on the ion's charge and coordination environment.

key_concept.definition: Ionic radius is half the distance between the nuclei of two adjacent ions in an ionic crystal, measured in picometers (pm) or angstroms (Å), where the total distance is partitioned between cation and anion based on established reference values.
key_concept.context: Ionic radius values depend on coordination number (number of opposite charges surrounding the ion), oxidation state, and measurement method. Standard values typically assume coordination number of 6.

classifications.value: Radius of positively charged ions (metals that lose electrons)
characteristics: Higher effective nuclear charge per electron
classifications.behavior: Size decreases with increasing positive charge; higher charge = smaller radius due to stronger nuclear attraction on fewer electrons.

examples.explanation: Na atom: 186 pm; Na⁺ ion: 102 pm. Loss of 3s¹ valence electron reduces electron cloud significantly, and remaining 10 electrons experience stronger pull from 11 protons.

image.alt: Size comparison of Na atom and Na⁺ ion
image.caption: Na⁺ is 45% smaller than Na atom due to electron loss and reduced shielding.

examples.explanation: Fe atom: 126 pm; Fe²⁺: 78 pm; Fe³⁺: 65 pm. Each additional electron lost further reduces size as remaining electrons are pulled closer by unchanged nuclear charge.

classifications.value: Radius of negatively charged ions (nonmetals that gain electrons)
characteristics: Lower effective nuclear charge per electron
classifications.behavior: Size increases with increasing negative charge; more added electrons = larger radius due to increased repulsion between electrons.

examples.explanation: Cl atom: 99 pm; Cl⁻ ion: 181 pm. Gain of one electron to fill 3p sublevel increases electron-electron repulsion, expanding the electron cloud by 83%.

image.alt: Size comparison of Cl atom and Cl⁻ ion
image.caption: Cl⁻ is 83% larger than Cl atom due to added electron and increased repulsion.

examples.explanation: O atom: 66 pm; O²⁻: 140 pm. Gaining two electrons to achieve noble gas configuration dramatically increases size due to electron repulsion in filled 2p⁶ sublevel.

classifications.value: Ions with same number of electrons but different nuclear charges
classifications.behavior: Within an isoelectronic series, ionic radius decreases as nuclear charge increases because more protons pull the same number of electrons closer.

examples.explanation: N³⁻ (171 pm) > O²⁻ (140 pm) > F⁻ (133 pm) > Na⁺ (102 pm) > Mg²⁺ (72 pm) > Al³⁺ (54 pm). All have 10 electrons (1s²2s²2p⁶), but radius decreases as protons increase from 7 to 13.

image.caption: Isoelectronic series: same electrons, different protons. More protons = smaller radius.

examples.explanation: P³⁻ (212 pm) > S²⁻ (184 pm) > Cl⁻ (181 pm) > K⁺ (138 pm) > Ca²⁺ (100 pm). All have 18 electrons, but increasing nuclear charge from 15 to 20 protons decreases radius.

components.definition: Ionic radius values are determined from X-ray crystallography measurements of ionic compounds, with standard reference values established for common ions.
components.interpretation: Cationic radius < atomic radius < anionic radius for the same element. Charge magnitude affects size: higher |charge| = more extreme size change.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Atomic and Ionic Radii
worked_examples.problem: Compare the sizes of the following pairs and explain the differences: (a) Na vs. Na⁺, (b) Cl vs. Cl⁻, (c) Fe²⁺ vs. Fe³⁺.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify whether each species is neutral atom, cation, or anion.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Na: neutral atom, Na⁺: cation; (b) Cl: neutral atom, Cl⁻: anion; (c) Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺: both cations

step_by_step.explanation: Apply cation/anion size rules.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Cation < atom; (b) Anion > atom; (c) Higher charge cation < lower charge cation

step_by_step.explanation: Look up or calculate expected size relationships.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Na (186 pm) > Na⁺ (102 pm); (b) Cl (99 pm) < Cl⁻ (181 pm); (c) Fe²⁺ (78 pm) > Fe³⁺ (65 pm)

step_by_step.explanation: Explain the electronic basis for each size difference.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Na⁺ loses valence shell; (b) Cl⁻ gains electron, increased repulsion; (c) Fe³⁺ has one fewer electron, same protons → stronger attraction

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Na > Na⁺ (cation smaller than atom); (b) Cl < Cl⁻ (anion larger than atom); (c) Fe²⁺ > Fe³⁺ (higher positive charge = smaller radius). These relationships reflect electron loss/gain and changes in electron-electron repulsion and effective nuclear charge.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Ionic radius relationships based on electron configuration and charge

sections.section_title: Why Cations Are Smaller Than Parent Atoms
sections.introduction: When atoms lose electrons to form cations, their size decreases significantly. This size reduction has important implications for ionic bonding, lattice energy, and the physical properties of ionic compounds.

key_concept.definition: Cations are smaller than their parent atoms because electron loss reduces electron-electron repulsion and may remove entire electron shells, allowing remaining electrons to be pulled closer to the nucleus.
key_concept.context: The magnitude of size reduction depends on how many electrons are lost and whether valence shell electrons are removed. Alkali metals show dramatic size reduction (losing entire valence shell); transition metals show more modest changes.

principles.statement: Removing electrons decreases repulsion between remaining electrons, allowing the electron cloud to contract.
principles.application: Explains why all cations are smaller than parent atoms, even when no complete shell is removed.
principles.example: Fe²⁺ (78 pm) vs. Fe³⁺ (65 pm): removing one more d-electron reduces repulsion, contracting the ion by 13 pm.
principles.analogy: Like removing people from a crowded room: fewer people (electrons) means each person has more space, but the group occupies less total area.

principles.principle: Increased Effective Nuclear Charge Per Electron
principles.statement: With fewer electrons but the same number of protons, each remaining electron experiences stronger effective nuclear attraction.
principles.application: Explains why remaining electrons are pulled closer to nucleus, reducing ionic radius.
principles.example: Na (11 protons, 11 electrons) → Na⁺ (11 protons, 10 electrons). Each electron in Na⁺ feels stronger average attraction.
principles.analogy: Like a magnet pulling fewer metal filings: same magnet strength (protons) pulling fewer filings (electrons) means each filing is pulled more strongly.

principles.statement: For main-group metals, cation formation often removes the entire valence shell, exposing the smaller core electron configuration.
principles.application: Explains dramatic size reduction for alkali and alkaline earth metals (40-50% size decrease).
principles.example: Na: [Ne]3s¹ (186 pm) → Na⁺: [Ne] (102 pm). Loss of n=3 shell exposes smaller n=2 core.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron loss and shell reduction in cation formation
image.caption: Cation formation removes valence electrons, often exposing smaller core electron shells.

principles.analogy: Like peeling an onion: removing the outer layer (valence shell) reveals a much smaller inner layer (core).

components.definition: The percentage size reduction for cations typically ranges from 30-60% depending on the element and charge.
components.interpretation: Higher charge cations show greater size reduction. Group 1 cations: ~45% reduction; Group 2 cations: ~50% reduction; Transition metals: ~30-40% reduction.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Cation Size Reduction
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the percentage size reduction when magnesium forms Mg²⁺. Atomic radius of Mg = 160 pm; ionic radius of Mg²⁺ = 72 pm.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the atomic and ionic radii.
step_by_step.calculation: r_atom = 160 pm; r_cation = 72 pm

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate the absolute size reduction.
step_by_step.calculation: Reduction = 160 - 72 = 88 pm

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate the percentage reduction.
Interpret the result.
step_by_step.calculation: Mg²⁺ is 55% smaller than Mg atom—more than half the size is lost when forming the cation

worked_examples.final_answer: Magnesium loses 55% of its atomic radius when forming Mg²⁺ (160 pm → 72 pm). This dramatic reduction reflects loss of the entire 3s² valence shell, exposing the smaller [Ne] core configuration. This size change significantly affects lattice energy and solubility of magnesium compounds.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Cation size reduction calculation and interpretation

sections.section_title: Why Anions Are Larger Than Parent Atoms
sections.introduction: When atoms gain electrons to form anions, their size increases significantly. This expansion affects ionic bonding distances, lattice energies, and the physical properties of ionic compounds containing anions.

key_concept.definition: Anions are larger than their parent atoms because added electrons increase electron-electron repulsion while nuclear charge remains constant, causing the electron cloud to expand.
key_concept.context: The magnitude of size increase depends on how many electrons are added and the initial size of the atom. Nonmetals show dramatic size increases (50-100% increase) when forming anions to achieve noble gas configurations.

principles.statement: Adding electrons increases repulsion between electrons in the valence shell, causing the electron cloud to expand.
principles.application: Explains why all anions are larger than parent atoms, with size increase proportional to number of added electrons.
principles.example: O (66 pm) → O²⁻ (140 pm): adding two electrons to 2p sublevel increases repulsion, expanding radius by 112%.
principles.analogy: Like adding people to a crowded room: more people (electrons) push against each other, requiring more total space.

principles.statement: The number of protons remains unchanged when forming anions, so the same nuclear charge must hold more electrons.
principles.application: Explains why effective nuclear charge per electron decreases, allowing electrons to occupy larger orbitals.
principles.example: Cl (17 protons, 17 electrons) → Cl⁻ (17 protons, 18 electrons). Each electron feels weaker average attraction.
principles.analogy: Like one teacher supervising more students: same teacher (protons) with more students (electrons) means less individual attention (attraction) per student.

principles.statement: Anions typically form by completing the valence shell to achieve noble gas configuration, which maximizes electron-electron repulsion in that shell.
principles.application: Explains why anions show consistent size increases within a group and why the increase is larger for elements gaining multiple electrons.
principles.example: F (71 pm) → F⁻ (133 pm): 87% increase; O (66 pm) → O²⁻ (140 pm): 112% increase. More electrons added = larger expansion.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron gain and shell expansion in anion formation
image.caption: Anion formation adds electrons to valence shell, increasing repulsion and expanding the electron cloud.

principles.analogy: Like inflating a balloon: adding air (electrons) increases pressure (repulsion), expanding the balloon (electron cloud).

components.definition: The percentage size increase for anions typically ranges from 50-120% depending on the element and charge.
components.interpretation: Higher negative charge anions show greater size increase. Group 17 anions: ~80-90% increase; Group 16 anions: ~100-115% increase; Group 15 anions: ~120%+ increase.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Anion Size Increase
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the percentage size increase when oxygen forms O²⁻. Atomic radius of O = 66 pm; ionic radius of O²⁻ = 140 pm.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the atomic and ionic radii.
step_by_step.calculation: r_atom = 66 pm; r_anion = 140 pm

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate the absolute size increase.
step_by_step.calculation: Increase = 140 - 66 = 74 pm

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate the percentage increase.
Interpret the result.
step_by_step.calculation: O²⁻ is 112% larger than O atom—more than double the size when forming the anion

worked_examples.final_answer: Oxygen increases 112% in size when forming O²⁻ (66 pm → 140 pm). This dramatic expansion reflects addition of two electrons to the 2p sublevel, maximizing electron-electron repulsion. This size change affects oxide compound lattice energies and explains why metal oxides have different structures than metal fluorides.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Anion size increase calculation and interpretation

sections.introduction: Ionic radius follows systematic trends across the periodic table, similar to but distinct from atomic radius trends. Understanding these patterns enables prediction of ion sizes, lattice energies, and compound properties.

key_concept.definition: Ionic radius increases down groups (additional electron shells) and varies across periods based on ion charge and position relative to the metal/nonmetal boundary.
key_concept.context: For ions with the same charge, trends mirror atomic radius trends. However, comparing cations to anions requires considering charge effects. Isoelectronic series provide the clearest periodic trend visualization.

principles.principle: Group Trend: Increasing Ionic Radius Down a Group
principles.statement: For ions with the same charge, ionic radius increases down a group as additional principal energy levels are added.
principles.application: Explains why Li⁺ < Na⁺ < K⁺ < Rb⁺ < Cs⁺ and F⁻ < Cl⁻ < Br⁻ < I⁻.
principles.example: Alkali metal cations: Li⁺ (76 pm) < Na⁺ (102 pm) < K⁺ (138 pm) < Rb⁺ (152 pm) < Cs⁺ (167 pm). Each adds a principal shell.

image.alt: Graph showing ionic radius increasing down groups for cations and anions
image.caption: Ionic radius increases down groups for both cations and anions, mirroring atomic radius trends.

principles.analogy: Like stacking rings: each new ring (electron shell) makes the stack (ion) larger.

principles.principle: Period Trend: Cations Decrease, Then Anions Increase
principles.statement: Across a period, cationic radius decreases (increasing nuclear charge), then anionic radius decreases among anions, but anions are much larger than cations in the same period.
principles.application: Explains the discontinuity in size when transitioning from metal cations to nonmetal anions within a period.
principles.example: Period 3: Na⁺ (102 pm) > Mg²⁺ (72 pm) > Al³⁺ (54 pm) << P³⁻ (212 pm) > S²⁻ (184 pm) > Cl⁻ (181 pm). Sharp increase at metal/nonmetal boundary.
principles.analogy: Like a cliff: gradual decrease on one side (cations), then sudden jump (anions much larger), then gradual decrease again.

principles.principle: Charge Effect: Higher Charge = More Extreme Size Change
principles.statement: For the same element, higher positive charge means smaller cation; higher negative charge means larger anion.
principles.application: Explains why Fe³⁺ < Fe²⁺ < Fe and why N³⁻ > O²⁻ > F⁻ in isoelectronic series.
principles.example: Iron: Fe (126 pm) > Fe²⁺ (78 pm) > Fe³⁺ (65 pm). Each additional electron lost contracts the ion further.

image.alt: Diagram showing charge effect on ionic radius for same element
image.caption: Higher positive charge = smaller radius; higher negative charge = larger radius for the same element.

principles.analogy: Like a spring: more compression (positive charge) = smaller; more expansion (negative charge) = larger.

components.definition: Ionic radius values for common ions range from ~25 pm (small highly-charged cations) to ~220 pm (large highly-charged anions).
components.equation: $\text{For isoelectronic series: } r \propto \dfrac{1}{Z}$ where Z = nuclear charge
components.interpretation: In isoelectronic series, radius is inversely proportional to nuclear charge. More protons pulling same electrons = smaller ion.

worked_examples.title: Ranking Ions by Size
worked_examples.problem: Arrange the following ions in order of increasing ionic radius: Ca²⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, S²⁻. Explain your reasoning.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine electron configurations and check if isoelectronic.
step_by_step.calculation: Ca²⁺: [Ar] (18 e⁻); K⁺: [Ar] (18 e⁻); Cl⁻: [Ar] (18 e⁻); S²⁻: [Ar] (18 e⁻). All isoelectronic!

step_by_step.explanation: Identify nuclear charges (atomic numbers).
Apply isoelectronic series rule: higher Z = smaller radius.
step_by_step.calculation: Size order: Ca²⁺ (20p) < K⁺ (19p) < Cl⁻ (17p) < S²⁻ (16p)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify with known values.

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing order: Ca²⁺ < K⁺ < Cl⁻ < S²⁻. All four ions are isoelectronic (18 electrons, [Ar] configuration), so size depends only on nuclear charge. Ca²⁺ has most protons (20) pulling 18 electrons, making it smallest. S²⁻ has fewest protons (16) holding 18 electrons, making it largest.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Isoelectronic series size ranking based on nuclear charge

worked_examples.title: Comparing Ionic Radii Across Periods and Groups
worked_examples.problem: Which is larger: Mg²⁺ or Ba²⁺? Which is larger: O²⁻ or Te²⁻? Explain using periodic trends.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify group relationships for each pair.
step_by_step.calculation: Mg and Ba: both Group 2; O and Te: both Group 16

step_by_step.explanation: Apply group trend: ionic radius increases down a group.
step_by_step.calculation: Ba is below Mg → Ba²⁺ > Mg²⁺; Te is below O → Te²⁻ > O²⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Consider principal quantum number difference.
step_by_step.calculation: Mg²⁺: n=2 core; Ba²⁺: n=5 core (3 additional shells); O²⁻: n=2; Te²⁻: n=5

step_by_step.explanation: Verify with known values.
step_by_step.calculation: Mg²⁺ (72 pm) < Ba²⁺ (135 pm); O²⁻ (140 pm) < Te²⁻ (221 pm)

worked_examples.final_answer: Ba²⁺ is larger than Mg²⁺ (135 pm vs. 72 pm); Te²⁻ is larger than O²⁻ (221 pm vs. 140 pm). Both pairs follow the group trend: ionic radius increases down a group due to additional electron shells. The size difference is substantial (~2×) due to 3 additional principal energy levels.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Group trends in ionic radius for cations and anions

sections.section_title: Isoelectronic Series and Nuclear Charge Effects
sections.introduction: Isoelectronic series—ions with the same number of electrons but different nuclear charges—provide the clearest demonstration of how nuclear charge affects ionic size. These series are essential for understanding periodic trends and predicting ion behavior.

key_concept.definition: An isoelectronic series consists of atoms and ions that have the same number of electrons and identical electron configurations, but different numbers of protons (nuclear charges).
key_concept.context: In isoelectronic series, ionic radius decreases as nuclear charge increases because more protons pull the same number of electrons closer to the nucleus. This provides a clean comparison without electron configuration variables.

principles.principle: Nuclear Charge Dominates in Isoelectronic Series
principles.statement: When electron count is constant, nuclear charge (Z) is the only variable affecting size. Higher Z = smaller radius.
principles.application: Enables precise predictions of relative ion sizes and explains why cations are smaller than anions in the same period.
principles.example: 10-electron series: N³⁻ (7p, 171 pm) > O²⁻ (8p, 140 pm) > F⁻ (9p, 133 pm) > Na⁺ (11p, 102 pm) > Mg²⁺ (12p, 72 pm) > Al³⁺ (13p, 54 pm).

image.alt: Graph showing inverse relationship between nuclear charge and ionic radius in isoelectronic series
image.caption: Isoelectronic series: as nuclear charge increases, ionic radius decreases inversely.

principles.analogy: Like magnets of different strengths pulling the same number of metal filings: stronger magnet (more protons) pulls filings closer.

principles.statement: In isoelectronic series spanning metals and nonmetals, there's a dramatic size jump between the largest cation and smallest anion.
principles.application: Explains why ionic compounds have specific structures and why lattice energies vary dramatically across periods.
principles.example: In 18-electron series: K⁺ (138 pm) to Cl⁻ (181 pm) shows 31% size increase despite same electron count, due to 2 fewer protons in Cl⁻.
principles.analogy: Like two teams with same players but different coaches: same team size (electrons), but different leadership (protons) changes team dynamics (size).

principles.statement: Smaller ions with higher charges have higher charge density (charge/volume), affecting hydration energy, lattice energy, and chemical reactivity.
principles.application: Explains why Al³⁺ has much higher hydration energy than Na⁺, and why small highly-charged ions polarize anions more strongly.
principles.example: Al³⁺ (54 pm, +3) has charge density ~3× higher than Na⁺ (102 pm, +1), leading to stronger ion-dipole interactions with water.

image.alt: Diagram comparing charge density of different ions
image.caption: Charge density = charge/volume. Small, highly-charged ions have high charge density and strong interactions.

principles.analogy: Like concentrating weight: same weight on smaller area (high charge density) creates more pressure (stronger interactions).

components.definition: Common isoelectronic series include the 2-electron (He-like), 10-electron (Ne-like), 18-electron (Ar-like), 36-electron (Kr-like), and 54-electron (Xe-like) series.
components.interpretation: Charge density scales with charge and inversely with cube of radius. Small changes in radius dramatically affect charge density.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing a Complete Isoelectronic Series
worked_examples.problem: Consider the 10-electron isoelectronic series: N³⁻, O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺. (a) Arrange in order of increasing radius. (b) Identify which has highest charge density. (c) Predict which has strongest hydration energy.

step_by_step.explanation: List nuclear charges for each ion.
step_by_step.calculation: N³⁻: 7p; O²⁻: 8p; F⁻: 9p; Na⁺: 11p; Mg²⁺: 12p; Al³⁺: 13p

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange by nuclear charge (higher Z = smaller radius).
step_by_step.calculation: Increasing radius: Al³⁺ < Mg²⁺ < Na⁺ < F⁻ < O²⁻ < N³⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Identify highest charge density (highest charge/smallest radius).
step_by_step.calculation: Al³⁺: +3 charge, smallest radius (54 pm) → highest charge density

step_by_step.explanation: Predict hydration energy (higher charge density = stronger hydration).
step_by_step.calculation: Al³⁺ has strongest hydration energy (most negative ΔH_hydration)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Increasing radius: Al³⁺ (54 pm) < Mg²⁺ (72 pm) < Na⁺ (102 pm) < F⁻ (133 pm) < O²⁻ (140 pm) < N³⁻ (171 pm). (b) Al³⁺ has highest charge density (+3 in smallest volume). (c) Al³⁺ has strongest hydration energy due to highest charge density, attracting water molecules most strongly. This demonstrates how nuclear charge controls size in isoelectronic series.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Isoelectronic series analysis for radius, charge density, and hydration energy

sections.introduction: Understanding ionic radius trends has practical applications in materials science, geology, biology, and chemistry. Ion size affects crystal structures, solubility, biological transport, and material properties.

key_concept.definition: Ionic radius trends are applied in predicting crystal structures, solubility patterns, biological ion selectivity, alloy formation, and material properties in ceramics, batteries, and catalysts.
key_concept.context: From designing better battery electrolytes to understanding kidney stone formation, ionic radius is a fundamental parameter that guides decision-making across scientific disciplines.

principles.statement: The ratio of cation to anion radii (radius ratio) predicts the coordination number and crystal structure of ionic compounds.
principles.application: Enables prediction of whether compounds will form NaCl-type, CsCl-type, ZnS-type, or other structures based on ion sizes.
principles.example: Radius ratio rules: 0.225-0.414 → tetrahedral (ZnS); 0.414-0.732 → octahedral (NaCl); >0.732 → cubic (CsCl). NaCl: r_Na⁺/r_Cl⁻ = 102/181 = 0.56 → octahedral ✓

image.alt: Diagram showing crystal structures predicted by radius ratios
image.caption: Radius ratio determines coordination number and crystal structure type for ionic compounds.

principles.analogy: Like packing spheres: size ratio determines how smaller spheres (cations) fit between larger spheres (anions).

principles.statement: Smaller ions with higher charges produce higher lattice energies, affecting melting points and solubility of ionic compounds.
principles.application: Predicts which ionic compounds will be soluble or insoluble; guides selection of salts for specific applications.
principles.example: MgO (Mg²⁺ 72 pm, O²⁻ 140 pm) has higher lattice energy than NaCl (Na⁺ 102 pm, Cl⁻ 181 pm), giving MgO much higher melting point (2852°C vs. 801°C).
principles.analogy: Like magnets: smaller, stronger magnets (high charge density ions) stick together more tightly (higher lattice energy).

principles.statement: Biological systems distinguish between ions based on size; ion channels and transport proteins are selective for specific ionic radii.
principles.application: Explains why Na⁺/K⁺ pumps distinguish between sodium (102 pm) and potassium (138 pm); why Ca²⁺ channels exclude Mg²⁺ despite same charge.
principles.example: Potassium channels have selectivity filter precisely sized for K⁺ (138 pm); Na⁺ (102 pm) is too small to interact optimally and is excluded despite being more abundant.

image.alt: Diagram of potassium channel showing size-based ion selectivity
image.caption: Ion channels use precise size matching to select specific ions, critical for nerve impulse transmission.

principles.analogy: Like a turnstile: only people of the right size can pass through; too small or too large and you're blocked.

principles.statement: Ions with similar radii (within 15%) can substitute for each other in crystal lattices, affecting mineral composition and alloy properties.
principles.application: Explains mineral solid solutions (olivine: Mg²⁺/Fe²⁺ substitution); guides alloy design for desired properties.
principles.example: Mg²⁺ (72 pm) and Fe²⁺ (78 pm) differ by 8%, allowing complete substitution in olivine (Mg,Fe)₂SiO₄. This creates mineral series with varying properties.
principles.analogy: Like interchangeable parts: similar-sized components can substitute in a machine without affecting function.

principles.statement: Ion size affects mobility in electrolytes; smaller ions move faster but may have stronger solvent interactions that slow them down.
principles.application: Guides selection of ions for battery electrolytes; explains why Li⁺ batteries dominate despite larger ions having potentially higher capacity.
principles.example: Li⁺ (76 pm) has high charge density, creating strong solvent interactions that reduce mobility. Na⁺ (102 pm) has lower charge density, potentially better mobility but lower voltage.

image.alt: Diagram showing ion mobility in battery electrolytes
image.caption: Ion size affects mobility in electrolytes: smaller ions have higher charge density but may move slower due to solvent interactions.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Crystal Structure from Ionic Radii
worked_examples.problem: Predict the crystal structure of CsCl using ionic radii: Cs⁺ = 167 pm, Cl⁻ = 181 pm. Calculate the radius ratio and determine the expected coordination number.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate the radius ratio (cation/anion).
Compare to radius ratio rules.
step_by_step.calculation: 0.92 > 0.732 → cubic coordination (coordination number = 8)

step_by_step.explanation: Predict crystal structure type.
step_by_step.calculation: Radius ratio > 0.732 → CsCl-type structure (body-centered cubic)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify with known structure.
step_by_step.calculation: CsCl does form CsCl-type structure with 8:8 coordination ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: CsCl has radius ratio 0.92, which exceeds 0.732, predicting cubic coordination (CN=8) and CsCl-type crystal structure. This matches the observed structure where each Cs⁺ is surrounded by 8 Cl⁻ and vice versa. The large Cs⁺ ion (167 pm) can accommodate 8 anions around it, unlike smaller Na⁺ which only accommodates 6.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Radius ratio rules for crystal structure prediction

worked_examples.title: Explaining Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Selectivity
worked_examples.problem: The sodium-potassium pump moves 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ into cells per ATP. Given Na⁺ radius = 102 pm and K⁺ radius = 138 pm, explain how the pump distinguishes between these ions.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate the size difference between ions.
step_by_step.calculation: Size difference = 138 - 102 = 36 pm (35% larger for K⁺)

step_by_step.explanation: Consider the binding site structure in the pump protein.
step_by_step.calculation: Na⁺ binding site: optimized for 102 pm; K⁺ binding site: optimized for 138 pm

step_by_step.explanation: Apply size-selectivity principle.
step_by_step.calculation: 35% size difference is large enough for protein to distinguish; optimal coordination geometry differs (Na⁺: 6-coordinate; K⁺: 8-coordinate)

step_by_step.explanation: Consider additional factors (charge density, hydration).
step_by_step.calculation: Na⁺ has higher charge density → stronger hydration → requires more energy to dehydrate for binding; K⁺ dehydrates more easily

worked_examples.final_answer: The Na⁺/K⁺ pump distinguishes ions based on their 35% size difference (102 pm vs. 138 pm). Binding sites are precisely sized for each ion with different coordination geometries. Na⁺ binding site is too small for K⁺; K⁺ binding site is too large for Na⁺. Additionally, Na⁺'s higher charge density means stronger hydration, requiring more energy to dehydrate for binding. This size-based selectivity is critical for maintaining cellular electrochemical gradients.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Ionic radius effects on biological ion selectivity and transport

sections.section_title: Relationship Between Ionic Radius and Other Properties
sections.introduction: Ionic radius correlates with multiple chemical and physical properties including lattice energy, hydration energy, melting point, and solubility. Understanding these relationships enables prediction of compound behavior from ion sizes.

key_concept.definition: Ionic radius is inversely related to lattice energy and hydration energy (smaller ions = stronger interactions), and affects melting point, solubility, and conductivity of ionic compounds.
key_concept.context: These correlations allow prediction of multiple properties from ionic radius data and explain why certain ionic compounds exhibit characteristic behaviors in different contexts.

principles.statement: Lattice energy is inversely proportional to the sum of ionic radii: smaller ions produce higher lattice energies due to closer ion-ion distances.
principles.application: Explains why MgO (small ions) has much higher melting point than NaCl (larger ions); predicts stability of ionic compounds.
principles.example: MgO: r_Mg²⁺ + r_O²⁻ = 72 + 140 = 212 pm, lattice energy = 3795 kJ/mol. NaCl: r_Na⁺ + r_Cl⁻ = 102 + 181 = 283 pm, lattice energy = 787 kJ/mol.

image.alt: Graph showing inverse relationship between ionic radius sum and lattice energy
image.caption: Lattice energy decreases as ionic radii increase due to weaker electrostatic attraction at larger distances.

principles.analogy: Like magnets: closer magnets (smaller ions) attract more strongly (higher lattice energy) than distant magnets (larger ions).

principles.statement: Hydration energy is inversely proportional to ionic radius: smaller ions have higher charge density and attract water molecules more strongly.
principles.application: Explains solubility trends; predicts which ions will be more strongly hydrated in aqueous solution.
principles.example: Li⁺ (76 pm): ΔH_hydration = -515 kJ/mol; Cs⁺ (167 pm): ΔH_hydration = -263 kJ/mol. Smaller Li⁺ is more strongly hydrated despite same +1 charge.
principles.analogy: Like a small magnet vs. large magnet with same strength: smaller magnet concentrates force (charge density), attracting metal (water) more strongly.

principles.statement: Melting point correlates with lattice energy, so smaller ions with higher charges produce higher melting points.
principles.application: Predicts thermal stability of ionic compounds; guides material selection for high-temperature applications.
principles.example: MgO (small ions, +2/-2): mp = 2852°C; NaCl (larger ions, +1/-1): mp = 801°C. Charge and size both affect melting point.

image.alt: Graph showing relationship between ionic radius and melting point for ionic compounds
image.caption: Melting point decreases as ionic radii increase due to lower lattice energies.

principles.statement: Solubility depends on balance between lattice energy and hydration energy; ion size affects both, creating complex solubility patterns.
principles.application: Explains why some small-ion compounds are insoluble (high lattice energy dominates); why some large-ion compounds are soluble (lower lattice energy).
principles.example: LiF (small ions): insoluble (high lattice energy); LiI (large anion): soluble (lower lattice energy). Same cation, different anion size changes solubility.
principles.analogy: Like a tug-of-war: lattice energy (holds solid together) vs. hydration energy (pulls ions into solution). Size affects both sides.

components.definition: The relationships between ionic radius and other properties can be expressed through Coulomb's law and Born-Landé equation for lattice energy.
components.equation: $\text{Lattice Energy} \propto \dfrac{q_+ q_-}{r_+ + r_-}$ where q = charge, r = ionic radius
components.interpretation: Lattice energy increases with ion charges and decreases with ion sizes. Both factors must be considered for accurate predictions.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Lattice Energy from Ionic Radii
worked_examples.problem: Compare the expected lattice energies of NaF, NaCl, and NaBr. Given: F⁻ = 133 pm, Cl⁻ = 181 pm, Br⁻ = 196 pm, Na⁺ = 102 pm. Which has highest lattice energy? Which has highest melting point?

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate the sum of ionic radii for each compound.
Apply lattice energy relationship (inversely proportional to radius sum).
step_by_step.calculation: Smaller sum → higher lattice energy: NaF > NaCl > NaBr

step_by_step.explanation: Predict melting point trend (correlates with lattice energy).
step_by_step.calculation: Higher lattice energy → higher melting point: NaF > NaCl > NaBr

step_by_step.explanation: Verify with experimental values.
step_by_step.calculation: NaF: 993°C; NaCl: 801°C; NaBr: 747°C ✓ Trend confirmed

worked_examples.final_answer: NaF has highest lattice energy and highest melting point because it has the smallest anion (F⁻ = 133 pm), giving the smallest ion-ion distance (235 pm). As anion size increases (F⁻ → Cl⁻ → Br⁻), lattice energy and melting point decrease. This demonstrates how ionic radius directly affects compound properties.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Ionic radius effects on lattice energy and melting point

worked_examples.title: Explaining Hydration Energy Trends
worked_examples.problem: Explain why Li⁺ has more negative hydration energy (-515 kJ/mol) than K⁺ (-321 kJ/mol) despite both having +1 charge. Given: Li⁺ = 76 pm, K⁺ = 138 pm.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare ionic radii of the two cations.
step_by_step.calculation: Li⁺: 76 pm; K⁺: 138 pm (K⁺ is 82% larger)

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate charge density (charge/volume) for each ion.
step_by_step.calculation: Li⁺: +1/(76)³; K⁺: +1/(138)³. Li⁺ has ~5× higher charge density

step_by_step.explanation: Apply hydration energy relationship (higher charge density = stronger hydration).
step_by_step.calculation: Li⁺ higher charge density → stronger ion-dipole interactions with water → more negative ΔH_hydration

step_by_step.explanation: Consider practical implications.
step_by_step.calculation: Li⁺ moves slower in solution despite smaller size due to larger hydration shell; affects battery performance

worked_examples.final_answer: Li⁺ has more negative hydration energy (-515 kJ/mol) than K⁺ (-321 kJ/mol) because Li⁺ is much smaller (76 pm vs. 138 pm), giving it ~5× higher charge density. The concentrated charge attracts water molecules more strongly, releasing more energy when hydrated. This also means Li⁺ carries a larger hydration shell in solution, affecting its mobility in batteries and biological systems.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Ionic radius and charge density effects on hydration energy

summary: Ionic radius measures ion size in crystal lattices, with cations smaller than parent atoms (electron loss reduces repulsion) and anions larger than parent atoms (electron gain increases repulsion). Cations: Na (186 pm) → Na⁺ (102 pm), 45% reduction. Anions: Cl (99 pm) → Cl⁻ (181 pm), 83% increase. Ionic radius increases down groups (additional shells) and varies across periods based on charge. Isoelectronic series (same electrons, different protons) show radius decreases as nuclear charge increases: N³⁻ > O²⁻ > F⁻ > Na⁺ > Mg²⁺ > Al³⁺ (all 10 electrons). Charge density (charge/volume) affects lattice energy, hydration energy, and chemical reactivity: smaller, highly-charged ions have higher charge density and stronger interactions. Applications include: crystal structure prediction (radius ratio rules), lattice energy estimation (inversely proportional to ion size sum), biological ion selectivity (Na⁺/K⁺ pumps distinguish 35% size difference), mineral substitution (ions within 15% size can substitute), and battery design (ion size affects mobility). Ionic radius inversely correlates with lattice energy and hydration energy (smaller ions = stronger interactions), affecting melting points and solubility. Understanding ionic radius trends enables prediction of compound properties, material behavior, and biological function based on ion sizes and charges.
